sources: ISSN Position Stand: Protein and Exercise (JISSN, 2017) — doi:10.1186/s12970-017-0177-8
Tipton KD: Nutrition for Injured Athletes (Biol Sport, 2015) — doi:10.5604/20831862.1134497
NIH ODS: Vitamin C, Zinc, Vitamin D, Calcium Fact Sheets
Close GL et al: Nutrition and Immune Function in Athletes (Proc Nutr Soc, 2021)
note: Dietary changes support recovery but do not replace medical treatment. Always follow the care team's instructions.
key_principle: Injury typically reduces activity but does NOT reduce protein or micronutrient needs — in many cases it increases them. Under-eating during recovery slows healing and risks excess muscle loss.

acute_inflammation.goal: Control inflammation, prevent excessive muscle loss, support immune response.
do: Maintain protein intake — do not reduce despite lower activity
Prioritise omega-3 rich foods to modulate inflammation
Ensure adequate vitamin C and zinc for immune support and tissue repair
Stay well hydrated — fluid supports tissue perfusion
avoid: Aggressively restricting calories — this is the #1 mistake during injury
High-dose antioxidant supplements in the first 48–72 hrs (may blunt the beneficial inflammatory signal)

repair_regeneration.goal: Collagen synthesis, muscle protein synthesis, bone/cartilage repair.
do: Protein 1.6–2.5 g/kg/day — higher end especially for muscle or tendon injuries
Vitamin C 200–500 mg/day from food — essential co-factor for collagen synthesis
Zinc from food — supports tissue repair and immune function
Calcium + Vitamin D for bone stress injuries
repair_regeneration.key_timing: Spread protein across 4–5 meals; 20–40 g per serving stimulates maximal MPS

return_to_training.goal: Rebuild muscle mass and strength lost during immobilisation.
do: Increase carbohydrates as activity resumes to fuel sessions
Creatine monohydrate 3–5 g/day — ISSN evidence supports accelerating strength return after immobilisation
Continue vitamin D and calcium for bone injuries

protein.target_g_per_kg: 1.6–2.5 (higher than maintenance due to increased MPS demand)
protein.why: Prevents muscle loss during immobilisation; drives tissue repair.

vitamin_c.target_mg_per_day: 200–500 from food (no evidence that megadoses help beyond this)
vitamin_c.why: Rate-limiting co-factor in collagen synthesis — critical for tendons, ligaments, cartilage.
vitamin_c.source: NIH ODS — Vitamin C Fact Sheet

zinc.target_mg_per_day: 8–11 (standard RDA; no evidence for therapeutic megadoses)
zinc.why: Cell proliferation, immune response, wound healing.

omega_3.target: 2–3 g EPA+DHA/day during acute inflammation phase
omega_3.why: Modulates inflammatory response; may reduce muscle loss during immobilisation (Close et al 2021).
omega_3.source: Close et al 2021; NIH ODS — Omega-3

vitamin_d_and_calcium.when_most_relevant: Bone stress fractures, shin splints, joint injuries
vitamin_d_and_calcium.source: NIH ODS — Vitamin D; Calcium Fact Sheets

collagen_precursors.why: Gelatin / hydrolysed collagen with vitamin C before exercise or physio improves collagen synthesis in tendons and cartilage.
collagen_precursors.dose: 15 g gelatin or collagen peptides + 200 mg vitamin C, 30–60 min before loading the injured tissue
collagen_precursors.vegetarian_note: Gelatin is not vegetarian. Vegan alternative: vitamin C-rich foods before physio; plant-based collagen boosters (Amla, bell pepper) to maximise endogenous production.
collagen_precursors.source: Shaw G et al, Am J Clin Nutr 2017

foods_to_prioritise.why: Protein + iron + zinc for tissue repair
Exceptionally high vitamin C for collagen (600 mg/fruit)
190 mg vitamin C per cup — pair with iron-rich foods

what_not_to_do: Do not aggressively cut calories — healing tissue needs energy
Do not skip protein because you are less active — MPS demand is actually higher
Do not rely on supplements over food unless a deficiency is confirmed by blood test
Do not use NSAIDs (ibuprofen) long-term — impairs collagen synthesis (medical note only)
